q: What is PID 1 on a modern Linux system, and why is it special?
a: PID 1 is the init system — today usually systemd. The kernel starts exactly one process (PID 1), and every other process descends from it, forming the process tree.

q: How does Linux create a new process to run a program?
a: fork() clones the current process, then the child calls exec() to replace its own program image with the new program. 'Run a command' = fork a copy, then become the new program.

q: What is a zombie (Z) process and how do you fix a pile of them?
a: A finished process whose parent hasn't read its exit status yet. You can't kill it (it's already dead) — you fix the buggy parent that isn't reaping its children.

q: Why prefer SIGTERM over SIGKILL?
a: SIGTERM is catchable — it lets a process flush buffers, finish requests, and clean up locks (graceful shutdown). SIGKILL (9) is uncatchable; the process gets no chance to clean up, risking data corruption or stale locks.
hint: One can be caught, one can't.

q: What does an exit code of 0 mean, and how do you see the last one in a shell?
a: 0 means success; non-zero means failure. The shell exposes the last command's exit code as $? . Shells, CI, and systemd all branch on exit codes.

q: What is a systemd 'unit' and how is its type indicated?
a: A unit is anything systemd manages, described by an INI-style file. The type is the filename suffix: .service, .socket, .timer, .target, .mount, .path.

q: Where should you put custom systemd units or overrides, and why not edit packaged ones?
a: Put them in /etc/systemd/system/ (highest priority) or use `systemctl edit` drop-ins. Packaged units in /usr/lib/systemd/system/ get overwritten on upgrade, so never edit them in place.

q: What are the three sections of a .service unit and what does each define?
a: [Unit]: metadata + dependencies/ordering (After/Before/Wants/Requires). [Service]: how to run it (ExecStart, Restart, User, Environment). [Install]: what enabling means (WantedBy target).

q: What's the difference between Wants= and Requires=?
a: Both pull in a dependency, but Requires makes your unit fail if the dependency fails (hard), while Wants is a soft wish (preferred for resilience). Neither alone sets order — use After/Before for that.

q: What does Restart=on-failure give you?
a: Automatic restart of a crashed service — turning any plain program into a self-healing daemon without writing daemonization logic. It's the host-level version of what orchestrators do for pods.

q: What is the difference between `systemctl enable` and `systemctl start`?
a: start runs the service right now (this boot only); enable makes it start automatically at boot. They're independent — `enable --now` does both.
hint: Classic 'why didn't it come back after reboot?' bug.

q: What's the difference between `systemctl reload` and `systemctl restart`?
a: reload runs the unit's ExecReload to re-read config with no downtime (if supported); restart kills and re-launches the process, dropping in-flight work. Prefer reload for config changes.

q: After editing a unit file, what must you run before your change takes effect?
a: systemctl daemon-reload (re-reads unit files from disk), then restart/reload the service. (`systemctl edit` runs daemon-reload for you.)

q: What's the fastest way to find broken services?
a: systemctl --failed (or list-units --state=failed), then `systemctl status <unit>` for the exit code/output, then `journalctl -u <unit>` for full logs.

q: Why does `systemctl stop` reliably kill all of a service's child processes?
a: systemd tracks every process of a service in its own cgroup, so it can stop the entire process tree — no orphaned children, unlike old init scripts.

q: Where do systemd services' logs go, and how do you read one service's logs?
a: Into the journal (systemd-journald captures stdout/stderr as structured entries). Read with `journalctl -u <unit>`; add -e (end), -f (follow), -n N (last N), --since.

q: How should a service under systemd handle logging?
a: Just write to stdout/stderr — the journal captures it with full metadata. No need to manage log files, rotation, or syslog. (Same 'log to stdout' contract containers use.)

q: How do you read logs from the PREVIOUS boot, and what's the prerequisite?
a: journalctl -b -1 (and `--list-boots` to see them). It only works if persistent journal storage is enabled (/var/log/journal exists); otherwise the journal is wiped on reboot.

q: How do you filter the journal to only error-level entries?
a: Use the priority flag: `journalctl -p err` (priority err and worse). Levels: 0 emerg … 3 err … 4 warning … 6 info … 7 debug; lower = more severe. Combine with -u and --since.

q: What are cgroups and what do they do?
a: Control groups — the kernel feature that limits and accounts for the collective CPU, memory, and I/O of a group of processes. Both systemd resource limits and containers rely on them.

q: What's the difference between MemoryHigh and MemoryMax in a systemd unit?
a: MemoryHigh is a soft cap — the kernel throttles and reclaims aggressively as the group approaches it. MemoryMax is the hard wall — exceeding it triggers the OOM killer inside the cgroup. Set High below Max for graceful pressure.

q: In kernel terms, what happens when a Kubernetes pod shows 'OOMKilled'?
a: The container's memory limit is enforced as a cgroup MemoryMax on the host; the process group exceeded that hard cap and the cgroup OOM killer terminated it. Container limits ARE cgroups.

q: Which systemd directives cap a service's CPU and memory?
a: MemoryMax / MemoryHigh for memory; CPUQuota (e.g. 50%) and CPUWeight for CPU; TasksMax to cap process/thread count. Apply live with `systemctl set-property`.

q: What is a namespace, and how does a user namespace enable rootless isolation?
a: A namespace gives a process its own view of one global resource (PID, mount, net, UTS, IPC, user, cgroup). A user namespace maps in-namespace UID 0 to an unprivileged host UID, so a process can be 'root' inside while harmless outside — enabling rootless containers.

q: What are Linux capabilities and why use them?
a: Root is split into ~40 capabilities, so a process holds only the slice it needs (e.g. CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE to bind ports <1024) instead of full root. Grant CAP_SYS_ADMIN reluctantly — it's nearly root.

q: Name three systemd directives that sandbox a service.
a: ProtectSystem=strict (mostly read-only FS), PrivateTmp=true (private /tmp), NoNewPrivileges=true (no privilege escalation), ProtectHome, CapabilityBoundingSet, SystemCallFilter (seccomp). `systemd-analyze security <unit>` scores how hardened a unit is.

q: How does MemoryHigh= differ from MemoryMax=?
a: MemoryMax is the hard cap — exceed it and the cgroup is OOM-killed. MemoryHigh is a soft throttle: the kernel reclaims aggressively above it, applying back-pressure before the hard kill, often avoiding the OOM killer entirely.

q: What is PSI and why is it a better saturation signal than utilization?
a: Pressure Stall Information (/proc/pressure/{cpu,memory,io}) reports the % of time tasks were STALLED waiting for a resource. It detects contention directly, whereas high utilization alone may not mean anything is actually waiting.

q: What is socket activation in systemd?
a: systemd opens the listening socket itself and starts the service only on the first connection (a paired .socket unit), and can hand the socket across restarts with zero dropped connections — saving resources and enabling on-demand start.

q: Why use systemd timers instead of cron?
a: Timers (.timer units) support calendar and monotonic schedules, missed-run catch-up (Persistent=true), randomized delays, and run the job as a normal unit — so output goes to the journal and it inherits resource limits/sandboxing.

q: How does the OOM killer choose a victim, and how do you influence it?
a: It picks by oom_score (roughly the biggest memory user), adjustable per process via oom_score_adj. A cgroup hitting MemoryMax is OOM-killed within that cgroup (Kubernetes' OOMKilled). Protect a critical process with a negative adj.

q: A service fails with 'too many open files' — where do you fix it durably?
a: Set the file-descriptor rlimit per service with LimitNOFILE= in the systemd unit, rather than relying on shell ulimit defaults that won't apply to the managed service.
